cards.front: When should you use parentheses with operators?
cards.back: Always use parentheses to disambiguate the precedence of operators if there is any question or opportunity for confusion. This improves code clarity.

cards.text: The remainder operator {{c1::%}} returns the {{c2::remainder}} from an integer division. For example, 7 % 3 = {{c3::1}}.

cards.front: Why should you prefer prefix increment/decrement over postfix?
cards.back: Prefix (++x) is more efficient because it doesn't create a temporary copy of the old value. Postfix (x++) must store the old value to return it.

cards.front: What is the rule about using variables with side effects in a statement?
cards.back: Do not use a variable that has a side effect applied more than once in a given statement. This causes undefined behavior.

cards.front: When is it acceptable to use the comma operator?
cards.back: Only in for loops. Otherwise, write separate statements for better readability.

cards.text: The conditional operator syntax is {{c1::condition ? expressionIfTrue : expressionIfFalse}}. Parenthesize the entire operator when used in {{c2::compound expressions}}.

cards.front: Why should you avoid using == and != with floating point numbers?
cards.back: Floating point rounding errors make exact equality comparisons unreliable. Use epsilon comparison instead: std::abs(a - b) < epsilon.

cards.front: What are the three logical operators and what do they do?
cards.back:
! (NOT) - negates a boolean
&& (AND) - true if both operands are true
|| (OR) - true if at least one operand is true

cards.question: Complete this function to return "apple" for 1, "apples" otherwise:
cards.explanation: Use the conditional operator to return one of two string values based on whether num equals 1.

Chapter 6 covers C++ operators and their proper use:
  • Use parentheses to clarify precedence
  • Prefer prefix increment over postfix
  • Avoid side effects in complex expressions
  • Don't use comma operator except in for loops
  • Use conditional operator for simple conditions
  • Never use == with floats
  • Leverage short-circuit evaluation for safety and efficiency
examples: (x > 0) ? a : b  // Conditional for simple cases

cards.text: Always prefer {{c1::prefix}} increment (++x) over {{c2::postfix}} (x++) because it's more {{c3::efficient}}.
Never use {{c1::==}} or {{c2::!=}} to compare floating point numbers. Instead, check if {{c3::std::abs(a - b) < epsilon}}.
